Quantum computing vs photonics computing
What is computing? A model that works well is
(i) represent some information in a physical system,
(ii) manipulate that system, and
(iii) read the state of the resultant system.
Computation is therefore governed by the type of physics it is using under the hood.
Classical computers use classical systems like transistors, which are governed by equations discovered centuries ago by the likes of Newton, Lagrange and Hamilton.
Quantum physics has an extra 'trick' called entanglement which enables a different type of parallelization. It's this feature which enables things like breaking RSA encryption via Shor's algorithm. But 'quantumizing' transistors into entangling qubits is tricky; useful hardware is many years away.
An exciting middle ground involves relaxing the idea of making a direct transistor analogue and using interfering systems of light waves to do computation instead ('photonics computing').
This approach is beginning to show promising speedups on important computational tasks, like the matrix multiplications which are pivotal to AI inference.
How to get exposure?
Before light can compute, it has to be generated, modulated, transmitted, routed, amplified, received and packaged. There are already a whole host of photonics companies that have specialized in this area for a long time, in other industry, and the hardware is well developed.

We are entering the next stage of our growth journey. Today, we proved to our early users and market makers that we are providing unprecedented value and modernizing traditional equity markets.
As of today, orderbook liquidity on QFEX has ~2.5x’d and spreads have compressed ~5x vs the last month. We expect further institutions to onboard this week, resulting in more and deeper liquidity, as well as the opening up of new markets beyond the US, Sweden and Korea, which we already have.
